# Phase 3: Dialogue Pressure Packet

This packet is currently grounded in the lessons extractable from `stories/dramione/dramione-1-original.md`.

The useful mechanics are not "copy this banter style." They are:

- exposition arrives through disagreement
- institutions become tangible through titles, files, rooms, and procedures
- every reply nudges status
- assessment and description happen through what one character notices about the other in the room

## Core goal

Make dialogue carry:

- pressure
- status
- information
- world logic

without flattening into briefing language.

## Build the conversation around leverage

Before writing the exchange, name the leverage.

Who currently has:

- the file
- the room
- the appointment
- the authority
- the information
- the refusal
- the social upper hand

If no one has leverage, the dialogue will drift.

## Let status start at the threshold

Pressure does not have to wait for the principals to sit down.

Useful threshold moves:

- a porter demanding the appointment before opening the gate
- a chart, ledger, or clipboard that decides who gets admitted
- a title correction that shifts the balance before the real topic begins
- the wrong hallway, concealed door, white coat, or insignia changing how the room reads

The institution should often announce itself before the main argument starts.

## Let institutions speak through objects and procedures

Grounded dialogue does not explain the institution from outside. It lets the institution show up through what people handle, correct, sign, request, or deny.

Useful institutional nouns:

- note
- file
- desk
- badge
- gate
- chart
- porter
- appointment
- case assignment
- report
- office
- lab
- title

The scene gets stronger when these are not just mentioned, but used.

Concrete institutional beats:

- a file slapped onto a desk
- a chart checked before entry
- a wand, seal, or badge displayed rather than explained
- a lab coat, gloves, or face covering making the work visible before anyone describes it

## Every line should move status

Strong pressure dialogue rarely trades neutral information.

Replies usually:

- correct
- challenge
- refuse
- reframe
- dismiss
- provoke
- test
- concede narrowly

If two consecutive lines merely exchange facts, add leverage back in.

## Use corrections as leverage

Repeated corrections can do as much status work as insults.

Examples:

- correcting a title
- correcting a rank
- correcting the name of the room or institution
- correcting whether the assignment is surveillance, protection, audit, or access

Each correction tells the reader who thinks they own the terms of the exchange.

## Thread physical beats between lines

Dialogue pressure is stronger when the room keeps participating.

Between spoken lines, use small beats that:

- show assessment
- reveal irritation or control
- register a prop
- remind us of the room
- adjust who owns the space

Good in-between beats include:

- taking the chair
- offering no handshake
- checking the file
- glancing at the door
- looking at the badge or title
- a folder dropped on the desk
- a long look at clothing, posture, or insignia
- a knee bouncing under the desk while the mouth stays controlled
- a hand pausing on the office door instead of fully entering
- a glance at the protective gear, notebooks, or stacked paperwork that defines the room

## Let exposition ride conflict

Instead of narrating how the system works, make the characters fight through it.

Good pressure-exposition questions:

- who assigned this?
- who authorized this?
- who gets access?
- who is refusing to cooperate?
- what procedure is being resisted?
- what title matters here?

The information should arrive because someone wants to push, resist, or reinterpret it.

## Wit is optional. Pressure is not

A dry or barbed tone can help, but it is not the core mechanic.

The real lesson is:

- competence under friction
- professionalism under strain
- history pressing on a formal exchange

Do not confuse "dialogue pressure" with "everyone must sound clever."

## Description through mutual assessment

When two people size each other up, description becomes part of the contest.

Use what one notices because it matters now:

- insignia
- robe, uniform, or coat
- hair too controlled or too loose
- boots, gloves, folders, ink, keys
- how someone sits
- whether they stand, offer a seat, or withhold one

That keeps description live and motivated.

The best details are usually the ones that change the stakes of the exchange:

- the Auror badge that makes the visit official
- the overstacked desk that proves exhaustion before it is admitted
- the trainers, coat, or gloves that show which world the speaker belongs to

## Dialogue-pressure checks

Ask:

1. What does each speaker want from the exchange right now?
2. What object, title, or procedure is carrying institutional force?
3. Does each reply move status, not just information?
4. Are the in-between beats helping the conversation, not pausing it?
5. Could the scene lose the institution and still play the same way? If yes, the world is underused.

## Common failure modes

- Dialogue becomes neutral Q&A
- Exposition gets moved into narration instead of conflict
- Room props exist but do no status work
- Characters sound interchangeable because no one is pushing differently
- The institution is named but not embodied in procedure, titles, or objects

## Before moving to Phase 6

You should have:

- named the leverage on both sides of the exchange
- made the institution tangible through objects, titles, or procedure
- ensured that most replies change status rather than only delivering facts
- used physical beats that keep the room active between lines
